            cell [e357] [cursor=pointer]
              generic "Skipped: Insufficient explicit relationship support in admissible source text. The technique description focuses on signal reception parameters and command blocking, while the countermeasure description focuses on fault management system vulnerabilities. No direct connection between signal strength targeting and fault management is explicitly stated in either node's description or the relationship note beyond the metadata linkage." [e358] [cursor=pointer]
              generic "DE-0003.04->CM0042 • create-qras-manifest-relationship-1776964279" [e359] [cursor=pointer]
                generic "DE-0003.04->CM0042" [e360] [cursor=pointer]
                generic "create-qras • create-qras-manifest-relationship-1776964279" [e361] [cursor=pointer]
            cell [e362] [cursor=pointer]
              generic "Return exactly one top-level JSON object with keys: - pairs - skipped_reason Generate up to 3 grounded, non-duplicative QRA pairs about the relationship between this SPARTA technique and this SPARTA countermeasure. ## Technique Technique ID: DE-0003.04 Technique Name: Command Receivers Received Signal Strength Technique Description: Threat actors may target the on-board command receivers received signal parameters (i.e., automatic gain control (AGC)) in order to stop specific commands or signals from being processed by the spacecraft. For ground controllers to communicate with spacecraft in orbit, the on-board receivers need to be configured to receive signals with a specific signal to noise ratio (ratio of signal power to the noise power). Targeting values related to the antenna signaling that are modifiable can prevent the spacecraft from receiving ground commands. ## Countermeasure Countermeasure ID: CM0042 Countermeasure Name: Robust Fault Management Countermeasure Description: Ensure fault management system cannot be used against the spacecraft. Examples include: safe mode with crypto bypass, orbit correction maneuvers, affecting integrity of telemetry to cause action from ground, or some sort of proximity operation to cause spacecraft to go into safe mode. Understanding the safing procedures and ensuring they do not put the spacecraft in a more vulnerable state is key to building a resilient spacecraft. ## Optional Relationship Note Relationship Note: SPARTA metadata lists countermeasure CM0042 Robust Fault Management in technique DE-0003.04 Command Receivers Received Signal Strength countermeasure_ids. ## Reminders - This prompt is about the relationship, not standalone node definitions. - Relationship Note, when present, is admissible structured SPARTA metadata establishing the direct linkage. - Do not infer relevance from IDs, naming, or hierarchy alone. - Every non-empty pair must have at least one quote from the technique and one quote from the countermeasure. - Relationship Note may establish the linkage but must not replace node evidence entirely. - If either side is non-substantive or placeholder-like, return zero pairs. - Preserve modality exactly. - Prefer fewer pairs over weak pairs. - If source support is insufficient, return zero pairs." [e363] [cursor=pointer]
            cell "—" [e364] [cursor=pointer]
            cell "Insufficient explicit relationship support in admissible source text. The technique description focuses on signal reception parameters and command blocking, while the countermeasure description focuses on fault management system vulnerabilities. No direct connection between signal strength targeting and fault management is explicitly stated in either node's description or the relationship note beyond the metadata linkage." [e365] [cursor=pointer]
              generic "Insufficient explicit relationship support in admissible source text. The technique description focu..." [e366] [cursor=pointer]
            cell "7766ms" [e367] [cursor=pointer]
            cell "deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V3.1-TEE" [e368] [cursor=pointer]
          row [e369] [cursor=pointer]
            cell [e370] [cursor=pointer]
              generic "Complete: CM0032 On-board Intrusion Detection & Prevention is relevant to DE-0003.02 Rejected Command Counter because threat actors may attempt to modify the Rejected Command Counter to hide their attack attempts, and the on-board IDS/IPS monitors mission critical components and responds to threats including evasion techniques. The IDS/IPS must integrate with traditional fault management to provide a holistic approach to faults, which could include detecting unauthorized modifications to system counters." [e371] [cursor=pointer]
              generic "DE-0003.02->CM0032 • create-qras-manifest-relationship-1776964279" [e372] [cursor=pointer]
                generic "DE-0003.02->CM0032" [e373] [cursor=pointer]
                generic "create-qras • create-qras-manifest-relationship-1776964279" [e374] [cursor=pointer]
            cell [e375] [cursor=pointer]
              generic "Return exactly one top-level JSON object with keys: - pairs - skipped_reason Generate up to 3 grounded, non-duplicative QRA pairs about the relationship between this SPARTA technique and this SPARTA countermeasure. ## Technique Technique ID: DE-0003.02 Technique Name: Rejected Command Counter Technique Description: Threat actors may attempt to hide their attempted attacks by modifying the onboard Rejected Command Counter. Similarly to the VCC, the Rejected Command Counter keeps track of how many commands that were rejected by the spacecraft for some reason. Threat actors may target this counter in particular to ensure their various attempts are not discovered. ## Countermeasure Countermeasure ID: CM0032 Countermeasure Name: On-board Intrusion Detection & Prevention Countermeasure Description: Utilize on-board intrusion detection/prevention system that monitors the mission critical components or systems and audit/logs actions. The IDS/IPS should have the capability to respond to threats (initial access, execution, persistence, evasion, exfiltration, etc.) and it should address signature-based attacks along with dynamic never-before seen attacks using machine learning/adaptive technologies. The IDS/IPS must integrate with traditional fault management to provide a wholistic approach to faults on-board the spacecraft. Spacecraft should select and execute safe countermeasures against cyber-attacks. These countermeasures are a ready supply of options to triage against the specific types of attack and mission priorities. Minimally, the response should ensure vehicle safety and continued operations. Ideally, the goal is to trap the threat, convince the threat that it is successful, and trace and track the attacker — with or without ground support. This would support successful attribution and evolving countermeasures to mitigate the threat in the future. “Safe countermeasures” are those that are compatible with the system’s fault management system to avoid unintended effects or fratricide on the system. ## Optional Relationship Note Relationship Note: SPARTA metadata lists countermeasure CM0032 On-board Intrusion Detection & Prevention in technique DE-0003.02 Rejected Command Counter countermeasure_ids. ## Reminders - This prompt is about the relationship, not standalone node definitions. - Relationship Note, when present, is admissible structured SPARTA metadata establishing the direct linkage. - Do not infer relevance from IDs, naming, or hierarchy alone. - Every non-empty pair must have at least one quote from the technique and one quote from the countermeasure. - Relationship Note may establish the linkage but must not replace node evidence entirely. - If either side is non-substantive or placeholder-like, return zero pairs. - Preserve modality exactly. - Prefer fewer pairs over weak pairs. - If source support is insufficient, return zero pairs." The IDS/IPS must integrate with traditional fault management to provide a holistic approach to faults, which could include detecting unauthorized modifications to system counters." [e377] [cursor=pointer]

              generic "Complete: Technique DE-0003.02 Rejected Command Counter involves threat actors modifying the onboard counter to hide their attack attempts from discovery. Countermeasure CM0044 Cyber-safe Mode provides a configuration-controlled and integrity-protected state where critical software and configuration are stored in memory with hardware-based controls and are not modifiable, which could prevent unauthorized modifications to counters like the Rejected Command Counter." [e384] [cursor=pointer]

            cell [e388] [cursor=pointer]
              generic "Return exactly one top-level JSON object with keys: - pairs - skipped_reason Generate up to 3 grounded, non-duplicative QRA pairs about the relationship between this SPARTA technique and this SPARTA countermeasure. ## Technique Technique ID: DE-0003.02 Technique Name: Rejected Command Counter Technique Description: Threat actors may attempt to hide their attempted attacks by modifying the onboard Rejected Command Counter. Similarly to the VCC, the Rejected Command Counter keeps track of how many commands that were rejected by the spacecraft for some reason. Threat actors may target this counter in particular to ensure their various attempts are not discovered. ## Countermeasure Countermeasure ID: CM0044 Countermeasure Name: Cyber-safe Mode Countermeasure Description: Provide the capability to enter the spacecraft into a configuration-controlled and integrity-protected state representing a known, operational cyber-safe state (e.g., cyber-safe mode). Spacecraft should enter a cyber-safe mode when conditions that threaten the platform are detected. Cyber-safe mode is an operating mode of a spacecraft during which all nonessential systems are shut down and the spacecraft is placed in a known good state using validated software and configuration settings. Within cyber-safe mode, authentication and encryption should still be enabled. The spacecraft should be capable of reconstituting firmware and software functions to pre-attack levels to allow for the recovery of functional capabilities. This can be performed by self-healing, or the healing can be aided from the ground. However, the spacecraft needs to have the capability to replan, based on equipment still available after a cyber-attack. The goal is for the spacecraft to resume full mission operations. If not possible, a reduced level of mission capability should be achieved. Cyber-safe mode software/configuration should be stored onboard the spacecraft in memory with hardware-based controls and should not be modifiable. ## Optional Relationship Note Relationship Note: SPARTA metadata lists countermeasure CM0044 Cyber-safe Mode in technique DE-0003.02 Rejected Command Counter countermeasure_ids. ## Reminders - This prompt is about the relationship, not standalone node definitions. - Relationship Note, when present, is admissible structured SPARTA metadata establishing the direct linkage. - Do not infer relevance from IDs, naming, or hierarchy alone. - Every non-empty pair must have at least one quote from the technique and one quote from the countermeasure. - Relationship Note may establish the linkage but must not replace node evidence entirely. - If either side is non-substantive or placeholder-like, return zero pairs. - Preserve modality exactly. - Prefer fewer pairs over weak pairs. - If source support is insufficient, return zero pairs."               generic "Complete: CM0044 Cyber-safe Mode is relevant to DE-0003.01 Vehicle Command Counter (VCC) because threat actors may modify the VCC to hide attack attempts from ground controllers, and cyber-safe mode provides a capability to enter a configuration-controlled and integrity-protected state using validated software and configuration settings when threatening conditions are detected. This known good state could help address VCC manipulation by restoring proper command counting functionality." [e423] [cursor=pointer]

              generic "Return exactly one top-level JSON object with keys: - pairs - skipped_reason Generate up to 3 grounded, non-duplicative QRA pairs about the relationship between this SPARTA technique and this SPARTA countermeasure. ## Technique Technique ID: DE-0003.01 Technique Name: Vehicle Command Counter (VCC) Technique Description: Threat actors may attempt to hide their attempted attacks by modifying the onboard Vehicle Command Counter (VCC). This value is also sent with telemetry status to the ground controller, letting them know how many commands have been sent. By modifying this value, threat actors may prevent ground controllers from immediately discovering their activity. ## Countermeasure Countermeasure ID: CM0044 Countermeasure Name: Cyber-safe Mode Countermeasure Description: Provide the capability to enter the spacecraft into a configuration-controlled and integrity-protected state representing a known, operational cyber-safe state (e.g., cyber-safe mode). Spacecraft should enter a cyber-safe mode when conditions that threaten the platform are detected. Cyber-safe mode is an operating mode of a spacecraft during which all nonessential systems are shut down and the spacecraft is placed in a known good state using validated software and configuration settings. Within cyber-safe mode, authentication and encryption should still be enabled. The spacecraft should be capable of reconstituting firmware and software functions to pre-attack levels to allow for the recovery of functional capabilities. This can be performed by self-healing, or the healing can be aided from the ground. However, the spacecraft needs to have the capability to replan, based on equipment still available after a cyber-attack. The goal is for the spacecraft to resume full mission operations. If not possible, a reduced level of mission capability should be achieved. Cyber-safe mode software/configuration should be stored onboard the spacecraft in memory with hardware-based controls and should not be modifiable. ## Optional Relationship Note Relationship Note: SPARTA metadata lists countermeasure CM0044 Cyber-safe Mode in technique DE-0003.01 Vehicle Command Counter (VCC) countermeasure_ids. ## Reminders - This prompt is about the relationship, not standalone node definitions. - Relationship Note, when present, is admissible structured SPARTA metadata establishing the direct linkage. - Do not infer relevance from IDs, naming, or hierarchy alone. - Every non-empty pair must have at least one quote from the technique and one quote from the countermeasure. - Relationship Note may establish the linkage but must not replace node evidence entirely. - If either side is non-substantive or placeholder-like, return zero pairs. - Preserve modality exactly. - Prefer fewer pairs over weak pairs. - If source support is insufficient, return zero pairs."
